narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a high family income background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 29. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 40.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejection as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure—it's notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position. On a more positive note, your high family income was the top factor pulling the prediction back toward passing, as it exceeds the average and signals strong support resources compared to most applicants. Your undergraduate GPA, while slightly below the dataset average of 3.21, also worked in your favor by nudging the odds toward success more than it hurt. Similarly, your LSAT score, though under the typical 36.7 average, contributed positively to the model's view of your potential. Your 1st decile in Year 1 grades added some concern, reinforcing the Year 3 signal, but it had less weight overall, while your full-time enrollment plan was a minor positive in line with the vast majority of students. In essence, your lower academic deciles in school were the key hurdles compared to peers who rank higher, overshadowing strengths like your family's financial stability. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a white male from a supportive background—it's the model's data-driven assessment of bar passage patterns. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger performance in remaining years, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and preparation. Consider targeted bar prep resources early, and reapply after addressing these gaps—many students turn predictions around with focused improvement. We're rooting for your success in law.
